questions.answer: This code snippet takes the float value of y divided by x and then checks to make sure if its not equal to itself. If the float is not equal to itself, and y is equal to x, the float is set to 1. Otherwise, its -1. If the difference of n subtracted from itself is not the same when done twice, then x is set to 0. Next, a series of checks determine if x or y are positive, if one or the other are positive, or if neither are positive. Depending on the outcome, the arctangent of the original float value is taken to determine what the proper trig value would be. At the end, the sum of x and y is returned.

questions.answer: This code snippet finds and returns the arc tangent of the quotient of two floats, x and y. Lines 4-7 adjust the values of n and x depending on how the division of y over x affects the values after the decimal point of n. Then, depending on whether x is positive or negative, the arc tangent of n is returned under the assumption that n is finite. If x is negative, it is also checked whether or not y is greater than or equal to zero. This check allows for the accounting of whether pi needs to be added or subtracted from the resulting arc tangent of n. If x is equal to zero (and the division comes out to undefined), the values of y are checked. If y is positive, the arc tangent returned is simply the sum of x (0) and half of pi. If y is negative, the arc tangent returned is simply the subtraction of x (0) and half of pi. If nothing has been returned thus far, the sum of x and y is returned to give the arc tangent of y/x.

questions.answer:
This code snippet finds the lowest positive root of some quadratic equation when the values a, b, and c are given. The values a, b, and c correspond to the quadratic equation that gives the roots:
x = (-b+-(sqr(b^2-4ac)))/(2a).
The determinant (b^2-4ac) is calculated. If the determinant is less than 0, there are no real roots, so Float.Nan is returned. Otherwise, the determinant is greater than or equal to 0, so there is at least one real root. The exact roots are then calculated as r1 and r2. If r1 is greater than r2, their values are swapped so that r1 is less than r2. Then, if one of them is positive, the value of that variable (r1 or r2) is returned [if r1 is negative and r2 is positive, r2 is returned despite being greater]. If neither are positive, then Float.Nan is returned so there is no positive root.
